original_abstract: Precise delivery and high drug loading remain significant hurdles in cancer chemotherapy. While self-assembling drug delivery systems (DSDSs) offer a promising alternative to traditional nanocarriers, maintaining supramolecular stability within the complex physiological environment of a living organism is challenging. Here, we report a light-activated anticancer palladium complex, PdL, that utilizes metallophilic Pd···Pd interactions to drive in vivo self-assembly. Density functional theory and experimental characterization confirm that these interactions facilitate the formation of stable nanostructures. In a human skin melanoma (A375) xenograft model, these self-assembled nanoparticles exhibit an exceptionally long blood circulation time (over 12 hours) and achieve high tumor accumulation (up to 10.2% of the injected dose per gram). Upon activation with green light, the complex triggers a potent type I photodynamic effect, generating superoxide radicals even under hypoxic conditions. This results in significant tumor destruction, evidenced by up to a 96-fold increase in cytotoxicity in vitro and robust anti-tumor efficacy in vivo. This study demonstrates that metallophilic self-assembly provides a powerful strategy for developing stable, high-loading supramolecular nanotherapeutics with superior tumor-targeting capabilities.
humanized_abstract: To date, achieving both accurate drug delivery and high drug loading remains a major challenge in cancer chemotherapy. Self-assembling drug delivery systems (DSDSs) have emerged as a new platform of nanocarriers for improving drug delivery, yet achieving long-term supramolecular stability in the in vivo environment is highly challenging. Here, we designed a light-activated anticancer palladium complex, PdL, which self-assembles into nanostructures through the assistance of metallophilic Pd···Pd interactions at the in vivo environment confirmed by both theoretical calculations and experiments. PdL-derived self-assembled nanoparticles (NPs) showed a remarkably long blood circulation time of over 12 h and reached as high as 10.2% injected dose per gram (ID/g) in human skin melanoma (A375) xenograft tumors. Upon activation with green light, PdL-induced a strong type I photodynamic effect and produced large amounts of reactive oxygen species (ROS), even under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions. In vitro, PdL exhibited up to a 96-fold enhancement of anticancer activity against A375 cells, while in vivo, PdL-induced significant tumor killing. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of using the metallophilic self-assembly approach to prepare stable and high-loading supramolecular nanotherapeutics with superior tumor-targeting efficacy.

Self-assembling drug delivery systems (DSDSs) have emerged as a new platform of nanocarriers for improving drug delivery, yet achieving long-term supramolecular stability in the in vivo environment is highly challenging. Here, we designed a light-activated anticancer palladium complex, PdL, which self-assembles into nanostructures through the assistance of metallophilic Pd···Pd interactions at the in vivo environment confirmed by both theoretical calculations and experiments. PdL-derived self-assembled nanoparticles (NPs) showed a remarkably long blood circulation time of over 12 h and reached as high as 10.2% injected dose per gram (ID/g) in human skin melanoma (A375) xenograft tumors. Upon activation with green light, PdL-induced a strong type I photodynamic effect and produced large amounts of reactive oxygen species (ROS), even under both normoxic and hypoxic conditions. In vitro, PdL exhibited up to a 96-fold enhancement of anticancer activity against A375 cells, while in vivo, PdL-induced significant tumor killing. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of using the metallophilic self-assembly approach to prepare stable and high-loading supramolecular nanotherapeutics with superior tumor-targeting efficacy.

In a human skin melanoma (A375) xenograft model, these self-assembled nanoparticles exhibit an exceptionally long blood circulation time (over 12 hours) and achieve high tumor accumulation (up to 10.2% of the injected dose per gram). Upon activation with green light, the complex triggers a potent type I photodynamic effect, generating superoxide radicals even under hypoxic conditions. This results in significant tumor destruction, evidenced by up to a 96-fold increase in cytotoxicity in vitro and robust anti-tumor efficacy in vivo.
